authors: Jenny Zhang, Shengran Hu, Cong Lu, Robert Lange, Jeff Clune
title: Darwin Gödel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
storage_format: Evolutionary archive of complete Python codebases of coding agents. Each agent is a repository with tools, prompts, and workflows. Parent selection is proportional to performance and inversely proportional to the number of children (open-ended exploration inspired by Darwinian evolution).
llm_used: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) (self-modification and SWE-bench evaluation), o3-mini (Polyglot evaluation)
performance_metrics: Percentage of solved tasks (resolve rate / pass@1)
baseline_compared: DGM w/o self-improve (fixed meta-agent, ADAS-style), DGM w/o open-ended exploration (latest version only), checked open-source SoTA (SWE-bench), Aider (Polyglot)
memory_size_over_time: Archive grows monotonically (all compilable agents are retained); ~80 agents generated in a complete run
main_result: DGM automatically improves a coding agent from 20.0% to 50.0% on SWE-bench Verified and from 14.2% to 30.7% on Polyglot via iterative self-modification with open-ended exploration. Improvements transfer across models (Claude 3.5→o3-mini, Claude 3.7) and benchmarks.
performance_vs_baseline: +30pp on SWE-bench (20%→50%); +16.5pp on Polyglot (14.2%→30.7%) vs base agent; comparable to human-designed open-source SoTA on SWE-bench
limitations: High computational cost (~2 weeks and significant API costs for one run); limited by the capabilities of the underlying FM; assumes that coding benchmarks reflect general self-improvement capability; self-modifications limited to prompts and workflows (does not train new models)
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified is a curated subset; evaluation on cost-scaled subsets; Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the sole self-modification model; inherent stochastic noise in LLM-based evaluations; the open-ended exploration process is not modifiable by DGM
